data.content:
Plan

Current findings
- The session selector in `src/components/agent-history-conversation-log.tsx` already shows a single conversation identifier as `#<index> <sessionId>` and does not need behavior changes.
- The pulldown entries users actually navigate are built in `src/components/interaction-picker.tsx` and rendered in `src/components/interaction-picker-ui.tsx`. Those entries currently label items as `Prompt #<n> · <source>` and show only workflow state plus time in the secondary row.
- Session data already contains everything needed for the request (`sessionId`, `startedAt`, `updatedAt`, prompt number, session ordering). No parser or backend changes are required.

Implementation plan
1. Extend the interaction item shape in `src/components/interaction-picker.tsx` to carry the conversation number/session identifier and the session date needed by the pulldown renderer. Keep selection IDs and sorting unchanged so dropdown behavior stays the same.
2. Add a focused label builder for pulldown entries that makes the conversation identifier primary and collapses redundant numbering into one value when prompt and take/conversation numbers are equivalent. Avoid showing both labels when they represent the same ordinal.
3. Update `src/components/interaction-picker-ui.tsx` so each pulldown option shows the conversation date alongside the existing time, while preserving the workflow-state metadata and current open/select interactions.
4. Keep all changes scoped to the history conversation-log pulldown path; do not alter the debug session selector, session cards, history parser, or unrelated history surfaces.

Test strategy
- Add or extend a component-level test around the history view / interaction picker rendering to assert that pulldown entries include the conversation date, still include the conversation identifier, and no longer duplicate Prompt/Take numbering when the values match.
- If the UI test surface is awkward, extract a small pure formatter helper and cover its combinations directly with Vitest for deterministic output.

Complexity and risks
- Complexity is low to moderate; the main risk is ambiguous date formatting in tests because locale/timezone-sensitive `toLocale*` output can be brittle. Prefer a single shared formatter with explicit date/time options or assertions that target stable substrings.
